generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with the weight of years, and with the knowledge that the night ahead may bring more than mere wind and rain—it may bring the end of all things. I have not spoken your name in five years, nor have I dared to think of you, for fear that the memory might bring forth more sorrow than I can bear. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to write, not to beg forgiveness, but to leave behind a final word.

You were once my pride, my joy, the light that burned brightest in my life. I have kept the lighthouse these many years, and in its glow I have found solace, yet I have never found the solace of your presence. I have watched the sea with its endless waves, and I have watched the sky with its ever-changing face, but I have never seen a horizon so distant, nor a silence so deep, as the one between us.

I have always believed that a lighthouse must stand firm against all tempests, that it must be a beacon to those who sail the dark. But I am not certain that I shall be able to stand. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with fury. I have heard the tales of men who have perished in such storms, and I fear that the night may take me before the dawn. Should I not survive, I beg

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*April 12th, 1897*  
*St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly*  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. It has been five long, silent years since you last came to see me, and I fear this may be the last letter I ever send. The storm is coming—dark and furious, like the storm of my own heart. The sea rages, the wind howls, and I can feel the old bones in my legs groaning beneath me. I am not sure I will make it through the night, and if I do not, I hope you will forgive me for not writing more often.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even in the darkest hours, you were the one who reminded me that there was still good in the world. I remember the day you left—how the wind was just as fierce, and the sky was just as dark. You said you needed to go, that you had your own path to walk. I did not argue. I did not try to hold you back. I simply watched you go, like the tide retreating from the shore.

I have tried to live my life here, in this lighthouse, with the sea as my only companion. The light still burns every night, as it has for thirty years, and I have kept

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with longing, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in all that time.

You were a child of light, always dancing in the glow of the lighthouse, your laughter like bells in the stillness of the night. I remember the day you first climbed the tower, your small hands gripping the iron railing, your eyes wide with wonder. You asked me if the sea ever cried, and I told you it did, in the way it crashes against the rocks, in the way it drowns the moonlight. You believed me, and I believed in you, even when the world outside these walls seemed to grow colder.

But then, you left. Not with a word, not with a farewell. You simply vanished, as if the sea had taken you away. I tried to reach you, to write, to call, but the distance was too vast, and the silence too deep. I told myself you were better off without me. That I was a man who had lost his way in life, a lighthouse keeper who had forgotten the light.

But now, as the storm approaches, I am beginning to wonder if I was

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**Whom I Have Not Seen in Five Years,**

**And Whom I Have Not Spoken To in Five Years,**

**I Write This with a Heavy Heart,**

**For the Storm is Upon Us,**

**And I Fear I May Not See Another Dawn.**

I sit here by the great lamp, its light flickering like a dying breath, and the wind howls as though it were the very devil himself. The sea roars, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes me tremble. I have seen many storms in my years, but none like this. The sky is black as ink, and the air is thick with the scent of salt and death. I do not know if I shall live to see the morning.

I have not written to you in five years, Eleanor. Five years, and yet the pain of your absence has never lessened. I have tried, I have tried, to forget, to move on, to let time heal what time cannot. But you remain in my mind, a ghost that haunts me in the quiet hours, when the lamp is still and the sea is calm.

I remember the day you left. You were not the same girl who once climbed the tower with me, who laughed at the sea and called me "Old Grizzle" with a smile that made my heart swell. You were not the same

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, the sea roiling as if it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that have stood for generations. The lantern flickers, though I've kept it burning, for I know the sea does not forgive the weak.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left. Five years since I last held you in my arms, since you called me "father" and then never again. I've tried to forget, but the nights are long here, and the silence of the sea is cruel. It echoes the silence between us.

I've always been a man of few words, but tonight, I feel the need to speak. Not for the sea, but for you. For the girl I once knew, who used to run through the dunes with her brother, who laughed at the stars and dreamed of the world beyond these rocks. I used to think I could give you everything—safety, love, a future. But you chose your own path, and I chose mine. And we both walked away.

But now... now I wonder if I was wrong. If I let the sea take more than it should have. If I let pride and silence drive a wedge between us that could not be mended. You were always my light, my daughter, and I let the storm blind me to that.

I don't know if I'll survive this night. The waves

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with a quivering hand, the wind howling like the cries of the damned, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern is lit, as it always is, but tonight, the light seems to flicker like a dying ember. I fear the storm may not pass, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I write, for I must, and for you, my dear child, I have so much to say.  

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor felt your touch. The silence between us has grown like the tide—rising, relentless, and cruel. I have tried to forgive, but the wounds remain. I have tried to forget, but the memories cling like barnacles to the hull of a ship.  

I have always been the keeper of the light, guiding the lost, the weary, the damned. But you, my Eleanor, were the light I once sought within myself. And I lost it. I lost you.  

I know you have found your own path, your own world. I know you have chosen a life apart from this one, and I would not have you return to a place where you are not wanted. But I have not forgotten you, and I have not stopped hoping. Even now, as the storm gathers, I find myself praying that the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you're reading this, then the wind must have already taken my breath, and the sea—ah, the sea—it has taken the rest. But I write this now, with the last flicker of the lighthouse lamp still burning, and the storm gathering like a beast on the horizon.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I've not spoken your name in all that time, and yet I think of you every night, as the waves crash against the rocks, and the wind howls through the tower like a ghost of your voice.

I know you've grown tired of waiting. You've moved on, as I knew you would. You were always the kind of girl who looked beyond the shore, beyond the horizon, beyond me. I was a man of stone and salt, bound to this place, and you were the light that once guided me—until I let it fade.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the sky is black, and the sea is wild. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me. I've been watching the weather for days, and the wind has been whispering things I've not heard in years. It speaks of the sea's fury, of the lighthouse's last stand, of a man who once had a daughter, and now has only the sea.

I've made my peace with the dark. But I cannot make peace with the silence between us.

I

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the heavens darken and the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, to speak, to say what I have long buried deep within me.

You were once my pride, my light in the darkest hours. I raised you with the same care I gave the beacon that guides the ships through these treacherous waters. But time, and distance, and silence have worn down the bonds we once shared. I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not spoken your name since.

You were angry, Eliza. Angry at me, at the world, at your own fate. I do not blame you for that. I was a man of stone and steel, a keeper of the light, and you were the flame that I could not contain. I tried to hold you close, to keep you safe, and in doing so, I smothered you. I did not understand that you needed more than a roof over your head and a meal in your belly. You needed freedom, and I gave you chains instead.

I have watched you go, and I have watched the world turn without you. I have not spoken to you, not even when your mother

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of finding you, but with the certainty that I must say these words before the night falls and the sea rises with a fury that no man has ever known.

Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I have been a stranger to you, though not by choice. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a solitary soul upon this bleak and wind-swept cliff, where the sea howls like a beast in pain and the stars are but distant whispers from the chaos below. I have kept the light burning, night after night, and yet I have not kept the fire of our love alive. That is the true darkness, Eliza—not the storm, but the silence between us.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if you will read these words, or if they will find their way to you through the sea and the wind. But I must say this, for the storm is coming, and I fear that I may not live to see the dawn.

Tonight, the sky is black as ink, and the wind has risen to a fury that shakes the very stones upon which we stand. The sea, once a placid companion, now roars like a wounded beast, and the waves crash upon the rocks with a violence that speaks of ancient wrath. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have prayed—prayed that the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this missive upon the creaking desk in the lighthouse, where the wind howls like a damned soul and the sea roars with a vengeance that even the most seasoned mariner would tremble to face. Five years have passed since our last words were exchanged, and in that time, the world has turned, and I, a man of stone and salt, have found myself more alone than ever.

You were once my light in the darkness, my solace when the tempests raged and the night was long. But now, you are a specter in my mind, a memory I dare not call to mind. I have tried, my dear, to forget, to let time mend the wounds of our estrangement. But time, like the sea, is fickle, and it has not softened the heart, nor the pain of what we have lost.

Tonight, the sky is black as a funeral shroud, and the wind howls with a voice that sounds like the wail of the damned. The storm is upon us, and I fear the waves may yet claim this old tower, and with it, my last breath. I have seen the signs—lightning crackling like the devil's own fingers, the sea rising in a fury that even the mightiest ship would flee. I have lived many years, Eliza, and I have known many storms, but none have ever felt as though

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in the quiet of your own hearth, by the firelight, with your children nestled close and your husband’s hand upon your shoulder. I write to you now, not with the calm of the summer nights, but with the weight of the storm that gathers over the cliffs, and the knowledge that the sea, though ever faithful, may yet take me before the morning light returns.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have counted each hour, each day, like the tolling of the bell that marks the turn of the tide. I have kept the lighthouse as you once taught me, with the same care and reverence, but the silence between us has grown like the salt upon the stones. I have not written, and I have not spoken, for fear that the words might not be enough, or that the words might not be welcome.

But tonight—tonight is different. The sky is a bruise of storm, and the wind howls like the wail of a soul in torment. The waves rise like the wrath of some forgotten god, and I feel the weight of the sea pressing upon me, as if it knows my time is near. I have been here for twenty-three years, Eleanor, and though I have seen many storms, none like this. I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

I have thought of you often, my dear. I have thought of

**April 12th, 1893**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it won’t find you at all. I’ve been trying to write to you for weeks now, but the wind howls like a banshee in this lighthouse, and the sea is so restless it feels like the world is ending. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. So I’m writing now, before it’s too late.

I’ve been thinking about you a lot lately. It’s strange, how the sea can be so quiet one moment and then roar like a beast the next, just like life can be. I’ve always felt like I was shouting into the void, but I guess that’s what it’s like when you’re alone in a lighthouse. That’s what it’s like when you’re alone in the world.

You know, I still remember the day you left. You were only ten, and I told you you’d be better off without me. I thought I was doing the right thing. I thought I was protecting you. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was so wrong. I didn’t realize how much you needed me, how much I needed you. I’ve been trying to make up for it ever since, but the years have passed like sand through my fingers, and I’ve been too afraid to reach for you

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five long years since your last letter, and I find myself writing this with trembling hands, for I have not spoken to you in such a time. The wind howls against the cliffs as I write, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. The storm is upon us, and I fear that I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you, nor have I sought you out, for I have been a fool, and you, my dear child, have been a ghost in my heart. I have let pride and silence build a wall between us, and I am now paying the price of my folly.

You were once my pride and joy, Eleanor. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes held a fire I could not extinguish, and how you turned your back on the life I had planned for you. I did not understand then, and I still do not fully understand now. But I do know this: you were never the cause of our estrangement. It was I who shut the door, and I who turned the key.

The lighthouse stands as it always has, a beacon in the dark, though I have grown weary of its light. I have tended the flame for many years, and now, as the storm gathers, I find myself questioning whether I have been the only one who has ever needed it.

I do not know if I will survive the night, Eleanor.

**Letter from a Victorian-era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1893*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it’s been a long while since I’ve heard of your well-being. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since the last time we spoke. You were so young then, and I so foolishly proud of you, thinking you’d never need a father’s guidance again. I was wrong. And I regret it more than words can say.

The storm is coming. I’ve seen the sky darken, the wind howl like a wounded beast, and the sea rise like a vengeful god. The waves have been growing fiercer by the hour, and the lighthouse, though sturdy, is not invincible. I’ve been keeping the light burning, as I always have, but I fear I may not be able to keep it burning much longer.

I’ve spent my life watching the sea, and I’ve seen many storms. But this one feels different. It’s not just the wind or the rain—it’s the silence. The silence of the world, the silence of our lives. I’ve been thinking a lot, Eleanor. Thinking of the years we lost, the words we never said, the love we never showed. I’ve been a fool, and I’ve been a father. And I’ve been alone.

I’ve written this letter in

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since stopped beating with the certainty it once did. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The storms that rage across the sea are nothing to the tempest within me.

The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the silence between us. I have watched the sea for decades, and I have seen many storms. But none have ever felt as though they might swallow me whole, as this one does. I fear I may not see dawn again, and if I do not, I must speak now—before the wind carries my words away like the last breath of a dying man.

You were young when you left, Eliza. So young that I thought I could still hold you close, still guide your steps. But you grew, and I did not. I stayed here, while you moved on. You chose a life of books and scholars, of quiet rooms and endless pages. I chose the sea, the salt, the solitude. And in choosing, we chose different paths. We did not part with words, only silence.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself longing for the warmth of your voice, for the way you used to read to me, the way your laughter once filled the

**To My Beloved Daughter,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since our last exchange, and now, as I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, I am gripped by a tempest that threatens not only the sea but the very soul of this old man.

You may not believe it, but I have not ceased to think of you. Each day, I walk the cliffs, gaze at the horizon, and in the stillness of the night, I hear your laughter echoing in the wind. I have kept a journal, Eleanor, filled with the names of the ships that pass, the storms that rage, and—most of all—the memories of you, my dear, my only child.

The wind howls now like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse, our sanctuary, trembles as though it might crumble beneath the weight of the heavens. I have lit the beacon, and it burns with a light that has guided countless vessels through the darkest nights. Yet tonight, I fear that even this light may not be enough.

I do not know if I shall see another dawn. And if I do not, I have one last wish: that you, my Eleanor, would come home. Not to this place, but to me. Not for the sake of duty or duty’s shadow

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the wind howling like a beast in the night, the sea roiling as if it seeks to swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern burns low, its light flickering like a dying soul. I have not seen you in five years, and I have not spoken your name in more. The world has moved on, and I have been left behind, like a lighthouse that has forgotten its purpose.

You were ten when you left, a child with fire in her eyes and a heart too full for her years. You said you were going to find your own way, to see the world beyond these rocks and the sea. I told you you'd come back, that this place was not the end of your story. But you did not return, and the years have passed like the tides, relentless and indifferent.

I have watched the waves crash against these cliffs for as long as I can remember. They have taken more than their share of lives, and I have learned to live with the silence. But tonight... tonight is different. The sky is black as ink, the wind a howl, and the sea is rising. I fear I may not see dawn.

I have kept this lighthouse for thirty years, and I have never once thought I would die here. But perhaps I have been wrong. Perhaps the sea has always known I would be its next victim. I have no regrets, not really. I have done

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, for the wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the sea roars as though it seeks to devour the very heavens. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not see me again. But if I do not, I must speak, for there are things I have never said, and I would not have them remain unsaid.

Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and in that time, I have seen the world shift like a ship in a tempest. I have watched the light of the lighthouse flicker and fade, and I have held it steady against the dark, as I have held steady against the storms of my own heart.

You were but a child when you left, and I did not know how to hold you. I thought I could wait, that time would mend what was broken. But time does not mend, Eliza. It only erodes. And I have eroded, as you have. We both have changed, and yet we have not changed at all.

I have not spoken to you, not because I have no words, but because I feared they would be too many. I feared they would be too much. I feared you would not hear them, or worse, that you would hear them and turn away again.

But now, as the tempest gathers strength and the sea threatens to claim the land

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is dark as a tomb. I’ve spent more years in this lonely place than I care to count, and yet, no night has ever felt as heavy as this one.

You were my light once—my only light. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes full of questions I never had the courage to answer. You were so young, so full of fire and fury, and I—God forgive me—I let you go. I thought I was protecting you. I thought I was doing the right thing. But you were right, weren’t you? I was afraid. Afraid of what I might say. Afraid of what I might become.

Five years have passed like a dream, and I have not spoken to you, not even in letters. I told myself I was waiting for the right moment, the right words. But the moment has passed. The words have been lost to the sea. And now, I fear, it is too late.

The storm is coming. The waves are rising, crashing against the rocks with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have lit the beacon, as I have always done, but I wonder if it will be enough. I wonder if this night will be the end

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and ink that is thick and dark as the night that now swallows the sea. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the waves crash against the cliffs as though the sea itself is crying out in grief. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet, tonight, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me like the very stones of this lighthouse.

You were a fine girl, Eliza. I remember the first time you came to see me, when you were but a child, no taller than the lamp itself. You stood at the edge of the platform, eyes wide with wonder, and I thought then that you would grow up to be something more than a mere girl. But time has a way of turning things to dust, and I fear that dust has settled upon us both.

I do not know if I will survive this storm. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as a crows’ nest. The wind howls with a voice that sounds like the ghost of your mother, and I can feel the cold seeping into my bones, as if the very air is mourning something. I have watched the sea for thirty years, and I have never seen it so wild, so furious. It is as if the world itself is coming to an end.

And yet, I think of you, Eliza.

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Lighthouse at Skerryvore]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**At the Lighthouse, Skerryvore**  
**October 12th, 1876**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and a mind clouded by the dread of the storm that looms on the horizon. It is not the wind that troubles me most, nor the sea, though both have shown their wrath in recent days. No, it is the thought of you, my daughter, who has not set foot in this house, nor in my life, for five long years.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left—leaving behind not only your father, but the life we had built together. I have watched the sea from these cliffs, night after night, and wondered if you would ever return. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, for the sake of those who sail these waters, and for the sake of my own conscience.

But tonight is different. The sky is black, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it seeks to swallow the land whole. The waves have already breached the lower walls, and the tide is rising with a fury I have never seen. I fear the lighthouse may not stand through this night. I fear I may not.

**To My Dearest, My Only Child,**

*May the stars above bear witness to this solemn missive, penned with trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence.*

I write to thee, my daughter, in the flickering light of the lighthouse’s lamp, which now casts its final, feeble glow upon the gathering storm. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea may be ever restless, it is but a pale echo of the tempest that rages within my soul.

I have stood here, alone upon this rocky height, for many a weary year, and the wind has carried my words to the shore, yet none have found their way to thee. I have not written, nor sent, nor called—though I have longed with aching heart to do so. The sea, thou knowest, is a cruel and fickle mistress, and so too has been the silence between us.

But tonight, the skies darken with a fury not seen in generations. The wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the waves rise with a vengeance that speaks of something greater than mere tempest. I fear that this night may be the last, and if I do not survive, I must speak my truth, for I have kept it buried too long.

I have not forgotten thee, my child. I have not ceased to pray for thy well-being, nor to dream of the day when we may once again stand together

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this missive finds you in robust health and with a heart not yet burdened by the weight of the world's sorrows. It has been five long years since we last exchanged words, and I must confess, I have often found myself seated by the fire, the glow of the lighthouse casting long shadows upon the walls, and wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the years have eroded even that memory.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a letter, for I have been afraid. Afraid that the words I might write would not be the right words, or that they would not reach you, or worse, that they would be met with silence once more. But now, as the storm gathers strength upon the horizon, and the sea roars like a beast unleashed, I feel a strange and sudden urge to speak, to be heard, to be known.

The wind howls through the lantern room like the wails of a damned soul, and the waves crash against the cliff with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this ancient tower. I have stood upon the platform, lantern in hand, and watched the tempest gather, and I have felt the weight of the world pressing in upon me. I have no doubt that the sea will claim us all, but I have also no doubt that if I do not speak now, I shall regret it for the rest of my days.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it won’t find you at all. I’ve been meaning to write for five years, but the sea has always been louder than my voice, and the wind has always been colder than my heart.

You were ten when you left, and I’ve counted every day since. I’ve watched the clock hands turn, and I’ve watched the tide come and go. You were the only thing that made this lighthouse worth keeping. Now, I wonder if it’s worth keeping *me*.

The weather’s been strange lately. The sky’s been a sickly gray, like the clouds are holding their breath. The waves have been coming in with more force than I’ve ever seen—like the sea is trying to swallow the land whole. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, though I’ve been so tired I’ve nearly fallen asleep at the wheel. But I can’t stop. Not now. Not when the storm’s coming.

I’ve been thinking a lot about you lately. I know you didn’t come back because you wanted to, and I know I didn’t ask you to. I was too stubborn, too proud. I thought I could protect you from the world, but I only ended up protecting myself from *you*. I was wrong. You were right to leave.

I’ve been reading the old journals again. They’re full of things I never told you—

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling the warmth of our once-cherished bond, but with the weight of a heart heavy with regret and the urgency of a soul on the verge of collapse. Five years have passed since our last words were exchanged, and in that time, I have come to understand the depth of the chasm between us. Yet, as the tempest gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to speak, not for reconciliation, but for the sake of my own peace.

The storm that now threatens to consume the coast is no mere gathering of winds and rain—it is a thing of wrath, of fury, and of dread. The sea, which has long been my constant companion, now churns with a violence I have never known. The lighthouse, which has stood as a beacon for countless vessels, now stands as a fragile witness to the chaos that threatens to claim us all. I fear that the night ahead may be the last I shall see.

I have spent these years in quiet solitude, tending to the flame that guides the lost and the wayward. Yet, in that flame, I have seen only my own reflection—a man who failed to see the light in the eyes of his child. I have not spoken, not once, for fear that my words would only deepen the wound. **Letter from a Victorian-era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1894*

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow corridors of this old stone tower. The sea roars against the rocks below, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, heavy with the weight of the storm that is upon us.
